attributes.stem:
A 79-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with a one day history of worsening abdominal pain. The pain is colicky and diffuse across the abdomen. She has been unable to tolerate food due to nausea and vomiting and cannot recall opening her bowels or passing wind over the last two days. Her medical history includes resection of a colorectal cancer five years ago, osteoporosis, depression, and polymyalgia rheumatica. Her current medications are calcium and vitamin D, paracetamol SR, prednisolone, fluoxetine, and docusate/senna.

On examination, her pulse rate is 120/min, blood pressure 90/60mmHg, respiratory rate 16/min, SpO2 96% on room air, and temperature 37.3C. Her abdomen is distended, with diffuse and mild tenderness to palpation. Tinkling bowel sounds can be heard.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following regular medications is  currently contraindicated in this patient?
attributes.explanation:
This patient has presented with an acute abdomen, most likely due to adhesion-obstruction. As with any patient admitted with an acute abdominal problem, consideration must be given to the possible need for some form of surgical intervention and how her current medications may have to be adjusted. For example, a patient on some form of anticoagulant therapy would probably need these to be withheld or modified.

In this case, it is safe to continue her calcium and vitamin D supplements. Likewise it safe to leave her taking paracetamol SR, however, she will likely require additional analgesia, given her current pain associated with the bowel obstruction. Fluoxetine can be continued. Sudden discontinuation of SSRIs can lead to antidepressant discontinuation syndrome. Prednisolone, as she has likely been on a long-term course of prednisolone in relation to her polymyalgia rheumatica. Sudden cessation of long-term glucocorticoids can precipitate an acute adrenal crisis which should be avoided.

Docusate/senna should be withheld. Senna acts as a laxative through its stimulating effects on the gut. It is contraindicated in patients who present with bowel obstruction due to this mechanism of action.

The route of administration of the various medications may need to be varied, depending on the need to keep the patient off any form of oral intake.
learningPoints: Suspect acute bowel obstruction in any patient with an acute abdomen, ongoing vomiting and inability to pass stools and flatus.
Stimulant laxatives such as senna should be avoided in all obstructed patients.
references: Frago R, Ramirez E, Millan M, Kreisler E, del Valle E, Biondo S. Current management of acute malignant large bowel obstruction: a systematic review. The American Journal of Surgery. 2014;207(1):127-138. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/24124659/

Noergaard M, Traerup Andersen J, Jimenez-Solem E, Bring Christensen M. Long term treatment with stimulant laxatives – clinical evidence for effectiveness and safety?. Scandinavian Journal of Gastroenterology. 2019;54(1):27-34. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/30700194/

Bancos I, Hahner S, Tomlinson J, Arlt W. Diagnosis and management of adrenal insufficiency. The Lancet Diabetes & Endocrinology. 2015;3(3):216-226. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/25098712/
Gabriel M, Sharma V. Antidepressant discontinuation syndrome. Canadian Medical Association Journal. 2017;189(21):E747-E747. Available at: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC5449237/
